hints: The pattern is r"(\d{4})-(\d{2})-(\d{2})". Three capture groups, in order: year (group 1), month (group 2), day (group 3).
In the replacement string, \1 refers to group 1 (year), \2 to group 2 (month), \3 to group 3 (day). To produce MM/DD/YYYY, the replacement is r"\2/\3/\1".

Use DATE_PATTERN.sub(r"\2/\3/\1", text) — sub replaces every match in the string and returns the result. Make sure the replacement is also a raw string (r"...") so the backslashes aren't eaten by Python.
If your output has the year first (YYYY/...) you have the group order backwards in the replacement. If month and day are swapped, check whether \2 is month and \3 is day in your pattern.
